questions.question: What is the most common type of ambulatory monitor, named after its inventor?
options: Telemetry monitor
Holter monitor
Transtelephonic monitor
Loop-memory monitor
questions.explanation: The Holter monitor is named after inventor Norman Holter. The name is often applied loosely to any type of ambulatory monitor, even though it's technically one specific type.

questions.question: During continuous ambulatory monitoring, what does the patient do when a symptom occurs?
options: Nothing — the tracing runs the same regardless of symptoms
Presses a button to place an event marker on the tracing
Calls the monitoring facility immediately
Removes the monitor and returns to the facility
questions.explanation: Continuous monitoring records the whole time the unit is worn. The event marker timestamps the exact moment a symptom occurred so the physician can correlate it with the diary entry later.

questions.question: How is intermittent recording different from continuous monitoring?
options: It only records while the patient is experiencing symptoms
It records for exactly 30 days regardless of symptoms
It requires five leads instead of three
It cannot be used with a patient diary
questions.explanation: Intermittent recording is shorter and faster to evaluate, but it only captures activity during symptoms — abnormal rhythms that happen just before symptoms start can be missed.

questions.question: What is the key difference between telemetry monitoring and transtelephonic monitoring?
options: Telemetry always requires a diary; transtelephonic never does
Telemetry is real-time monitoring, typically within a healthcare facility; transtelephonic transmits over a phone line, cell tower, or wifi and is often used for long-term outpatient monitoring
Telemetry uses ten leads; transtelephonic uses three
There is no meaningful difference between the two
questions.explanation: Telemetry sends a continuous signal to a monitoring station in real time, usually inside a facility, so no diary is needed since staff are watching directly. Transtelephonic monitors are portable and can transmit remotely for 30+ days.

questions.question: A postsymptom event monitor is worn like a wristwatch or handheld device. What does it primarily record?
options: A continuous 12-lead tracing for 48 hours
Heart activity for short periods immediately after the patient experiences symptoms
Only pacemaker battery status
Blood pressure alongside ECG data
questions.explanation: It's used mainly to document dysrhythmias lasting more than a few seconds, like atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, and supraventricular tachycardias, by capturing the activity right after symptoms hit.

questions.question: A loop-memory monitor can hold up to how many minutes of ECG tracing in memory?
options: 1 minute
5 minutes
15 minutes
60 minutes
questions.explanation: The 5-minute memory window means activating the monitor after an episode locks in the tracing from before, during, and after the event — useful for pinpointing the cause of syncope.

questions.question: Why might a patient need ambulatory monitoring even after already having a normal 12-lead ECG?
options: The 12-lead ECG is never accurate for cardiac patients
Ambulatory monitoring evaluates the heart over an extended period, so it can catch abnormal rhythms that a one-time ECG might miss if the patient wasn't symptomatic at that moment
Ambulatory monitoring replaces the need for a stress ECG entirely
Insurance requires both tests for every patient
questions.explanation: A 12-lead ECG captures a single point in time. If the patient's symptoms are intermittent, they may not show up during that brief test — ambulatory monitoring extends the window to 24-48 hours or longer.

questions.question: Besides diagnosing dysrhythmias, what are two other common uses of ambulatory monitoring mentioned in the chapter?
options: Measuring blood glucose and evaluating kidney function
Evaluating the effectiveness of antidysrhythmic medications and checking artificial pacemaker function
Screening for lung disease and measuring oxygen saturation
Diagnosing broken bones and soft tissue injuries
questions.explanation: Ambulatory monitoring can confirm whether antidysrhythmic drug therapy is working, and it's also used to evaluate pacemaker function after implantation or if problems arise.

questions.question: Which patient would NOT be a good candidate for ambulatory monitoring, according to the chapter?
options: A patient recovering from a myocardial infarction
A patient with dementia who cannot understand the process
A patient with an implanted pacemaker
A patient experiencing intermittent palpitations
questions.explanation: The patient must be able to understand and cooperate with the monitoring process — for example, maintaining an accurate diary — which a patient with dementia may not reliably be able to do.

questions.question: What should you tell a patient about bathing while wearing a Holter monitor?
options: Showers and tub baths are fine as long as the unit is wrapped in plastic
Only a sponge bath is allowed — no shower or tub bath, since the equipment must not get wet
Bathing must be avoided entirely for the full monitoring period
Only tub baths are allowed, not showers
questions.explanation: The recorder and electrodes must stay dry, so a sponge bath is the only option while the monitor is in place.

questions.question: What should a patient avoid during ambulatory monitoring because it can interfere with the tracing?
options: Reading and light exercise
Magnets, metal detectors, high-voltage areas, and electric blankets
Drinking water
Wearing loose-fitting clothing
questions.explanation: These sources of electromagnetic interference can distort the ECG signal being recorded, so patients are instructed to steer clear of them for the duration of monitoring.

questions.question: Why is a front-buttoning shirt recommended for patients during ambulatory monitoring?
options: It looks more professional for the diary photo
It's loose-fitting for comfort and reduces artifact, and it gives easy access to the electrodes and lead wires
It keeps the monitor warmer
It's required by the monitor manufacturer
questions.explanation: Loose-fitting clothing reduces artifact and discomfort, and a front-buttoning shirt makes it easy for staff to access the chest for lead placement and periodic checks.

questions.question: A patient asks what to do if an electrode becomes loose during monitoring. What is the correct instruction?
options: Ignore it — loose electrodes don't affect the results
Press the loose electrode back down in the center to reapply it; if it comes off completely, report it and return to the facility for replacement
Remove all electrodes and reapply them from scratch at home
Tape over all electrodes preemptively to prevent this
questions.explanation: Minor looseness can be fixed by pressing the electrode center to reseat it. A fully detached electrode requires a trip back to the facility, since it can't be reliably reapplied without proper skin prep.

questions.question: What should the patient record in the Holter diary?
options: Only symptoms that seem serious
All usual and unusual activities and symptoms, including timing — routine and strenuous activity, medication, emotional upsets, and any physical sensations, whether or not they seem important
Only the start and end time of the monitoring period
Only meals and sleep times
questions.explanation: The diary is only useful if it's complete. The chapter emphasizes: if in doubt, write it down — the physician needs to correlate the ECG tracing with what the patient was actually doing at each point.

questions.question: How should you approach explaining the ambulatory monitoring procedure to a pediatric patient?
options: Use the same technical language you'd use with an adult
Consider the child's age, use terms they can understand, let them touch the equipment beforehand to ease anxiety, and be sure to instruct the parent as well
Skip the explanation since children won't remember it anyway
Only speak to the parent and exclude the child from the conversation
questions.explanation: Reducing a pediatric patient's fear starts with age-appropriate language and letting them handle the equipment first. The parent still needs full instructions since they'll be supporting the child through the monitoring period.

questions.question: Before placing electrodes on a hairy site, what is the correct skin prep step?
options: Wet shave the area with soap and water
Dry shave the site, since wet shaving leaves a soap film that interferes with electrode adhesion
Skip shaving and place the electrode directly over the hair
Use only alcohol, never shave the site
questions.explanation: Wet shaving leaves a soap film that keeps electrodes from adhering well, so dry shaving is used instead — followed by abrading the skin to improve contact.

questions.question: For telemetry monitoring specifically, why is clipping chest hair recommended over shaving?
options: Clipping is faster, that's the only reason
Shaving increases irritation and scratching, which can cause artifact on the tracing
Clipping is required by all monitor manufacturers
Shaving is not allowed under any circumstances in a healthcare facility
questions.explanation: For telemetry, clipping instead of shaving reduces skin irritation. An irritated, itchy site can lead to scratching that shows up as artifact on the ECG tracing.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'stress loop' when securing electrode lead wires?
options: It helps the patient relax during the procedure
It takes up slack in the lead wire, preventing tension on the wire and electrode during patient movement
It's a decorative element with no functional purpose
It measures the patient's stress level
questions.explanation: By leaving slack looped and taped down, the electrode isn't pulled or tugged when the patient moves, which helps prevent an inaccurate tracing caused by lead tension.

questions.question: Why should electrodes be snapped onto the lead wires before being placed on the patient?
options: It's purely for organizational convenience, no patient benefit
It reduces pressure and discomfort on the patient's chest that would occur from attaching leads after the electrodes are already placed
It's required so the monitor can calibrate
It prevents the electrodes from expiring
questions.explanation: Attaching the lead wire to the electrode first, then placing the assembled unit on the patient, avoids pressing on the patient's chest while fumbling to connect leads afterward.

questions.question: After placing all electrodes and attaching the cable, what should be done before letting the patient put their shirt back on?
options: Immediately send the patient home
Run a baseline ECG tracing on the electrocardiograph to confirm the tracing is correct and the machine isn't malfunctioning
Remove the electrodes and start over
Skip straight to starting the digital recorder
questions.explanation: Confirming a good baseline tracing before the patient dresses and leaves catches problems — like a bad electrode or faulty lead — while they're still easy to fix.

questions.question: What should you do first when a patient returns to have their ambulatory monitor removed?
options: Immediately disconnect all lead wires
Briefly review the completed diary with the patient before removal
Send the digital disk to the lab before speaking with the patient
Ask the patient to fill out a new diary
questions.explanation: Reviewing the diary with the patient first lets you catch gaps or unclear entries before the equipment comes off, while the patient can still clarify details.

questions.question: If a patient arrives for monitor removal without their diary, what is the impact?
options: No impact — the ECG tracing alone is sufficient for interpretation
The results of the recording cannot be properly evaluated, since the physician needs to correlate the tracing with the patient's activities and symptoms
The monitor data is automatically deleted
The patient must repeat the entire test regardless of the diary
questions.explanation: The ECG tracing alone isn't diagnostic — it has to be matched against what the patient was doing and feeling at each point in time, which is exactly what the diary provides.

questions.question: Once monitoring is complete and results are sent to an outside reference laboratory, how long does it typically take to get the report back?
options: Same day
24-48 hours
7 to 10 days
30 days
questions.explanation: The chapter notes it usually takes 7 to 10 days for a reference laboratory to return results, which then get placed in the patient's chart for the physician to share with the patient.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a possible abnormal result from ambulatory monitoring?
options: Electrical-conduction defects in the heart's rate and rhythm-controlling system
Rhythm abnormalities
Premature atrial or ventricular contractions
Elevated white blood cell count
questions.explanation: White blood cell count is a blood test finding, not something an ECG-based ambulatory monitor detects. The monitor is focused on the heart's electrical rhythm.

questions.question: During the application procedure checklist, in what order should these two steps happen: (A) insert the digital disk and batteries into the recorder, (B) place electrodes on the patient's chest?
options: A before B — prepare the recorder before touching the patient
B before A — electrodes go on first, equipment prep happens after
Order doesn't matter
They must happen at the exact same time
questions.explanation: Per the procedure checklist, electrode placement and running a baseline tracing come first; preparing the recorder with a fresh digital disk and batteries happens afterward, right before starting the recording.
